title: Part 10
text: of ceremonies in rites of passage or formative experiences. This meaning again survives in a series of modern words like ‘premier’, ‘prince’, ‘principle’, ‘priest’, ‘primordial’, or ‘primitive’. Linguists emphasize that the original meaning implied a concrete, spatial rather than temporal sequence, which is in full congruence with van Gennep’s framework, where he places the spatial or territorial passage before all other ritual passages in the presentation of the ethnographic material (Chapter 2 of his book, we remember, is entirely dedicated to the ritual passing of spatial thresholds). Even further, *per derivatives also capture the idea of ‘birth’, or to ‘bring into the world’, most importantly in ‘parent’, but also as in the Italian partorire (give birth to); possibly even ‘father’ (pater). Szakolczai finally suggests that a derivate word, ‘part’, even captures the background horizon of any experience-passage: the ‘home’, from which and towards which any passage is performed as a taking part in something, or as an experience of ‘participation’. In short, the related etymology is extremely dense and Turner was certainly on the right track when opening up the analysis in this direction. Turner realized that liminality served not only to identify the importance of in-between periods, but also to understand the human reactions to liminal experiences: the way in which personality was shaped by liminality, the sudden foregrounding of agency, and the sometimes dramatic tying together of thought and experience. Turner came to identify his own project with the philosophy of Dilthey for this very reason (see Turner’s two most important discussions of Dilthey in 1982: 12–19; 1988: 84–97). This was indeed an important intellectual encounter, made late in Turner’s life (see also the discussion by Szakolczai 2004: 69–72; here Szakolczai explicitly develops the argument towards an ‘experiential sociology’). Turner immediately perceived an affinity between his own work and Dilthey’s, arguing that it takes an anthropologist to understand the significance of Dilthey (Turner 1985b: 210). Via Dilthey, Turner returned to the perhaps greatest question of modern philosophy: namely experience and how to understand experience. On the one hand, experience had been a cornerstone of both Cartesian and Kantian thought, hence of modern philosophy as such. On the other hand, there was something deeply problematic about modern philosophy and its hold on experience – or rather, its non-hold. And this was exactly what Dilthey had realized. In a certain sense, Turner was therefore trying to take up the ‘impossible’ Kantian task that Durkheim had pursued, namely of providing the a priori with ethnographic backing. Turner was himself not very explicit about what he was involved in, and to an extent it remained at an embryonic stage. As he wrote shortly before he died, ‘I don’t want to become involved in Dilthey’s philosophical speculations’ (1988: 90). But involved he was, and there is every reason to make explicit what was going on in order to pursue it further: The basis of knowledge does indeed have a social foundation, but the ‘social’ itself is no longer that abstract, reified collective consciousness represented and reproduced Liminality and the Modern 88 by undifferentiated primitives in the Durkhemian weltbild: it is the social drama performed by individuals who in the ritual act bring to the fore, and elaborate, cultural essentials. The exercise of tying together ethnography and philosophy has now been dramatically redefined. And this again was done much more in the spirit of van Gennep than in the spirit of Durkheim. Against the Kantian framework, Dilthey maintained that knowledge is based on structures of experience that are at once cognitive, affective and volitional – all of which contribute to the ‘form’ of the actual performance itself (as nicely summed up by Turner himself, 1988: 55). Following Turner’s own suggestions, it seems meaningful to suggest that much of our conceptual thought derives from human elaborations of in-betweenness. The importance of Dilthey rested with his revolt against Kantian and neo- Kantian thinking. In Kant’s worldview, the world itself is chaotic and unordered, and it takes a transcendental mind to make sense of it; but even this is a hopeless task as we can never know the ‘thing-in-itself’. Dilthey intuited that human experience is not chaotic and random, to be ‘constructed’ and ordered by the abstract categories of the transcendental mind; rather, experience has a structure of its own. But what was the nature of this structure? What were the ‘structures of experience’ that Dilthey kept insisting on? Elaborating van Gennep’s work, Turner identified these with the triadic, sequential and processual structure of rites of passage at the core of anthropology. Turner actually solved these problems, only his efforts were buried in a few posthumous essays. As succinctly put by Szakolczai (2009: 147), ‘[t]he solution is the recognition that the sequential order of a rite of passage is the structure of lived experience’. This dialogue across empirical ethnography, anthropological concept formation and continental philosophy is of utmost relevance, far more promising and deep-reaching than Durkheim’s forced empiricism – and we need to give it more attention than has so far been the case. The Kant-Durkheim genealogy, in all its later, various off-shots into nominalism, structuralism, Marxism and social constructivism, needs to be replaced by a Nietzsche-van Gennep/Dilthey-Turner genealogy. This has importance far beyond the anthropology of performance; it has importance for how we approach thought, knowledge and experience and how they interconnect in critical junctures. And we can now attempt to tie together the knots in a more comprehensive analytical framework, singling out, more schematically and as directly as possible, the possible spatio-temporal dimensions of liminality and the complementary concepts that serve to energize its analytical and diagnostic value. Chapter 4 Dimensions of Liminality This chapter attempts to pave further analytical ground, with and beyond Victor Turner, towards the application of liminality within a context of modernity, and in three ways: first, by proposing a descriptive model and a ‘typology’ that identifies various spatial and temporal dimensions of liminal situations; second, by suggesting a series of complementary concepts that facilitate a meaningful application of the concept of liminality with reference to processes of change and transition within larger social settings; third, by indicating a disciplinary and methodological framework for the kind of meaningful dialogue it is possible to construct between anthropology and social theory. Types of Liminality In Turner’s famous expression, liminality refers to any ‘betwixt and between’ situation or object. It is evident that this understanding opens up space for possible uses of the concept even beyond Turner’s own suggestions. Single moments, longer periods, or even whole epochs can be considered liminal. Liminality can also be applied to both single individuals and to larger groups (cohorts or villages), or whole societies, and arguably even entire civilizations. These various dimensions of liminality can be spelled out very simply. As we have suggested elsewhere (Thomassen 2009), and which we can now further elaborate and exemplify, experiences of liminality can be related to three different types of subjecthood: 1. single individuals 2. social groups (e.g. cohorts, minorities) 3. whole societies, entire populations, ‘civilizations’ The temporal dimension of liminality can relate to: 1. moments (sudden events) 2. periods (weeks, months, years) 3. epochs (decades, generations, arguably even centuries) These different dimensions can function together in a variety of combinations. This descriptive model can be suggested: Liminality and the Modern 90 Model 1 Types of Liminal Experiences: Temporal Dimensions Subject Time IndividualGroupSociety Moment Sudden event affecting one’s life (death, divorce, illness) or individualized ritual passage (baptism, ritual passage to womanhood, as for example among the Ndembu) Ritual passage to manhood (almost always in cohorts); graduation ceremonies, etc. Ritualized passage within a cosmological event-calendar, such as New Year, Harvest, Solstice. A whole society facing a sudden event (sudden invasion, natural disaster, a plague) where social distinctions and normal hierarchy disappear Carnivals Revolutionary moments Period Critical life-stages Puberty or teenage Ritual passage to manhood, which may extend into weeks or months in some societies Group travels Wars Revolutionary periods Epoch (or life- span duration) Individuals standing outside society, by choice or designated Monkhood. In some tribal societies, individuals remain ‘dangerous’ because of a failed ritual passage Twins are permanently liminal in some societies Religious Fraternities, Ethnic minorities, Social minorities, Transgender Immigrant groups betwixt and between old and new culture Groups that live at the edge of ‘normal structures’, often perceived as both dangerous and ‘holy’ Prolonged wars, enduring political instability, prolonged intellectual confusion (example: the Thirty Years’ War) Incorporation and reproduction of liminality into social and political structures Modernity as ‘permanent liminality’? It should of course be stressed that these are analytical distinctions of a somewhat arbitrary nature. There is no definitive way of distinguishing ‘moments’ from ‘periods’, and the dimensions invoked could also be thought of as a continuum. Moreover, while this scheme identifies types of liminal experience, it by no means follows that all these experiences are demarcated with a transition rite – at least not the same kind of clearly recognizable and institutionalized rites with identifiable masters of ceremony, as studied in the work of van Gennep. Before we invoke further examples of these dimensions, one further layer of complexity must be added, referring to spatial levels. Dimensions of Liminality 91 Dimensions of Spatial Liminality Liminality is very essentially a spatial concept. It is far from coincidental that van Gennep started his own analysis of ritual passages with a full chapter on ‘the territorial passage’, the title of Chapter II which followed immediately upon his initial classification of rites. Van Gennep clearly saw territorial border zones or border lines, thresholds or portals, as structurally identical with the intermediate period of a ritual passage: spatial and geographical progression correlates with the ritual marking of a cultural passage. Moreover, van Gennep even indicated that perhaps the physical passage of a threshold somehow preceded the rites that demarcate a symbolic or ‘spiritual’ passage: ‘A rite of spatial passage has become a rite of spiritual passage’ (van Gennep 1960: 22). It should be said very explicitly that van Gennep’s work represents a starting point for theorizing space, but also more concretely still, for conceptualizing borders and boundaries. Van Gennep refers to a vast gamut of ethnographic and historical data in his discussion of border crossings in Rites of Passage. In this context he discussed both concrete thresholds such as portals or doorways, but he also included demarcations of tribal societies, villages and towns, and neutral zones between countries (ibid.: 17). In other words, liminal places can be specific thresholds; they can also be more extended areas, like ‘borderlands’ or, arguably, whole countries, placed in important in-between positions between larger civilizations. Staying with the above threefold classification, we suggest that the spatial dimensions of liminality can relate to: 1. specific places, thresholds (a doorway in a house, a line that separates holy from sacred in a ritual, specific objects, in-between items in a classificatory scheme, parts or openings of the human body); 2. areas, zones and ‘closed institutions’ (border areas between nations, monasteries, prisons, sea resorts, airports); 3. countries or larger regions, continents (meso-potamia, medi-terranean; Ancient Palestine, in between Mesopotamia and Egypt; Ionia in Ancient Greece, in between the Near East and Europe). In introducing this third dimension we are going beyond van Gennep’s own suggestions and his initial framework of analysis. But there are strong grounds to suggest that even Karl Jaspers’ famous theory of the axial age can be meaningfully understood with the notion of liminality. Karl Jaspers’ description of the axial age in places used a vocabulary that is almost identical to the one originally proposed by van Gennep. Jaspers described the axial age as an in-between period between two structured world-views and between two rounds of empire building (1953: 51); it was an age of creativity where ‘man asks radical questions’, and where the ‘unquestioned grasp on life is loosened’ (ibid.: 3); it was an age of uncertainty and contingency: an age in which old certainties had lost their validity and where new Liminality and the Modern 92 ones were still not ready. It was a period in which individuals rose to the test and new leadership figures rose. In particular, the axial age gave birth to a new sub- stratum of persons: ‘free-standing’ intellectuals. One of the characteristics of these persons, as stressed by Jaspers, was the fact that they were most often wandering ascetics and therefore spatially uprooted (see Eisenstadt 1982 for the comparative analysis of these figures). Finally, and importantly for our purpose here: referring to the spatial co-ordinates, the axial ‘leaps’ all happened in in-between areas between larger civilizations, in liminal places: not at the centres, nor beyond reach of the main civilizational centres, but exactly at the thresholds, and that quite systematically so in the Eastern Mediterranean, China and India (for a fuller discussion of the axial age thesis and its relevance for contemporary globalization, see Thomassen 2010). Liminality in Large-scale Settings As described by van Gennep (1960) and Victor Turner (1969), during ritual passages, the initiands or the neophytes are brought to question most radically their own sensory apparatus, just as they challenge social order by setting themselves apart from any normally accepted social rules from their ‘betwixt and between’ position; they become nameless, timeless and socially ‘unstructured’, existing in a floating state of being, even as they acquire throughout the liminal period the necessary knowledge and experience in order that their transformed beings may eventually re-enter society and take up their new roles, which are recognized and stamped onto them in the re-aggregation ritual. Liminality is therefore a paradoxical state, both at the individual and the societal level. At the level of the individual, it is the destruction of identity, while at the level of society it involves the suspension of the structure of social order. However, whether in case of rituals or crises, the aim is to return to conditions of stability and normality. This happens by forging a new identity in the individual case, reflecting a shift of one’s position within the social order; while in the case of society new common bonds are formed through the cathartic experience of communitas (Turner 1969). Both processes involve the social and the asocial, and re-draw the boundaries between them. This book is mostly concerned with the consequences of such liminal states and moments at the societal level: the cases of liminal transformation to be discussed in the chapters to follow all deal with liminal figurations and social processes at a large-scale level. In other words, what primarily concerns us here are the processes in play in the third column in Model 1: natural disasters, carnival, revolutions, wars, periods of war (such as the Thirty Years’ War), events that somehow effect larger groups and not just single individuals or cohorts moving from one life- stage through to another. Moreover, what concerns us here are primarily liminal dynamics that had effects within larger areas and territories – eventually, somehow stamping Western modernity itself. Dimensions of Liminality 93 We are of course not the first to employ the concept of liminality in such a direction. The reoccurrence of experiences of liminality during large-scale political or civilizational disorders, has, among others, been explored in the work of Eisenstadt (1978). This idea in fact has to do with a little known dialogue across anthropology and social theory, namely that between Victor Turner and Shmuel Eisenstadt. Eisenstadt’s comparative-historical approach to the study of civilizations was much influenced by his Weberian reading of the ‘symbolic anthropologists’ and in particular his collaboration with Victor Turner in the early 1980s, when they jointly organized a seminar on ‘Comparative Liminality and Dynamics of Civilizations’, leading to a series of publications (see in particular Eisenstadt 1995). Eisenstadt realized that the concept of liminality could re-address the question of change and continuity in large-scale-settings also. As discussed by Boy and Torpey (2013), Eisenstadt proposes a framework for the systematic analysis of the impact of axial revolutions on the social structure that is heavily influenced by Edward Shils’s concept of the normative centre. Axial revolutions, Eisenstadt suggests, can be studied at three points: the emergence, the conceptualization and the institutionalization of the ‘basic tension between the transcendental and mundane orders’ (Eisenstadt 1982: 296). Like Shils, Eisenstadt conceives of the origins of social orders as residing in charisma; the main carriers of charisma are ‘autonomous intellectuals’. Some of Eisenstadt’s ideas have been further elaborated in several works of Arpad Szakolczai (1998, 2000). In fact, the most direct application of Turner’s ideas to large-scale political change has, on this note, been offered by Szakolczai in his analysis of communism, which he considers one particular form of ‘permanent liminality’. Employing again van Gennep’s tripartite structure, Szakolczai has argued that there are three types of permanent liminality, critically originating in the three phases of the rites of passage. ‘Liminality becomes a permanent condition when any of the phases in this sequence [of separation, liminality and re-aggregation] become frozen, as if a film stopped at a particular frame’ (2000: 220). He invokes a salient example for each type of permanent liminality: monasticism (with monks endlessly preparing for their separation from the world), court society (with individuals continuously performing their roles in an endless ceremonial game), and Bolshevism (as exemplifying a society stuck in the final stage of a ritual passage). The first two suggestions build on the insights of Turner himself, Max Weber (and his study of the Protestant ethic), Norbert Elias (and his study of court culture) and Michel Foucault. The understanding of communism as a specific ‘third stage’ type of permanent liminality can be sustained by pointing to the fact that ‘communism was a regime in which the Second World War never ended’ (ibid.: 223; Horvath and Szakolczai 1992). Rather than healing the wounds and looking to the future, communist regimes sustained themselves by playing continuously on the sentiments of revenge, hatred and suffering, ‘preventing the settling down of negative emotions’ (Szakolczai 2000: 223). This also meant that communist societies were inherently prone to continuous schisms and scapegoating mechanisms. But the obvious question is whether these logics can be confined to Liminality and the Modern 94 communism or are not rather, as Girard would suggest to us, an inherent feature of modernity, even in its liberal forms, and especially with respect to the current global age (Girard 2011). Natural Disasters as Macro-Liminality In the above model, referring to large-scale liminality in ‘moments’, we also invoked the example of sudden disasters, which can involve a larger population and throw a whole society into ‘forced’ and (initially) non-ritualized liminality. Concerning natural disasters, such as floods or earthquakes, such events will normally come as sudden ruptures and last for brief but dramatic moments. Logically, one would also assume that such disasters almost by definition cannot involve entire civilizations, bound in space and time as they are. However, there are numerous examples where this has indeed been the case, and such moments, even if localized and densely temporalized, can have lasting effects far beyond the given space and time. In fact, Minoan culture, this deep well of Europe’s cultural heritage, developed in close affinity with earthquakes and floods – and it was finally undone by a devastating flood and its consequences. With respect to European modernity, one event immediately presents itself as the perfect candidate for exemplifying large- scale liminality rupturing in a single moment: the Lisbon Earthquake of 1755. Let us briefly consider this event as a liminal moment with larger social consequences, to exemplify the mechanisms at play. The other dimensions of societal large-scale liminality (revolutions, wars, longer periods of war and political unrest) will be taken up explicitly in the chapters that follow. The Lisbon Earthquake of 1755 Oh, miserable mortals! Oh wretched earth! Oh, dreadful assembly of all mankind! Eternal sermon of useless sufferings! Deluded philosophers who cry, ‘All is well’, Hasten, contemplate these frightful ruins, This wreck, these shreds, these wretched ashes of the dead; These women and children heaped on one another, These scattered members under broken marble; Voltaire, Poem of the Lisbon Disaster, or, An Examination of that Axiom, ‘All is Well’ The Lisbon earthquake is without doubt the most dramatic natural disaster in modern European history. It is one of those sudden events that presents itself as a rupture into every level of the taken-for-granted. It occurred, incredibly enough, on November 1, All Saints’ Day, in many ways the most holy of all holy days, at around 09:40 local time, just as masses of people were assembled for prayer in the Dimensions of Liminality 95 city’s churches. Twelve-metre high waves slammed at the city, destabilizing even the major church towers which collapsed and crashed through roofs, crushing the people praying below. The brothels, located further up the hills, were spared – a fact that did not go unnoticed in the subsequent attempts to make sense of the event. Fires broke out at several places, merging into a veritable firestorm. Those who were not killed outright or trapped in the rubble, ran in panic down to the quays along the Tejo River, only to be swept away by one of the largest tsunamis ever generated in the Atlantic Ocean. The artistic presentations of the event leave us no doubt about it: this was experienced as the ultimate cataclysm, the disappearance of everything that one had been holding on to; this was Hell on Earth. It must be remembered that Lisbon was then one of the five largest cities in Europe. As a harbour town and Portugal’s capital, it was an extremely important trade hub, and still one of the most important passage points between Europe and its colonies. Most of its approximately 275,000 inhabitants were devoutly Catholic; the cathedrals and churches in town were filled to the brim when the earth started to tremble. It is still the worst earthquake (technically speaking a seaquake) recorded in Western history. Some claim that one quarter of the city’s inhabitants were wiped out in no time. Numbers are still disputed, but it is a fair estimate that approximately 70,000 people died instantly – an enormity. No-one was prepared for it – and there were no masters of ceremony to hold on to. This was a local event, but it sent shock-waves throughout Europe – and this Tardean metaphor of ‘waves’ becomes relevant here for several reasons. First, it was something literally felt far away from the epicentre of the catastrophe, damaging towns and villages from Morocco to France. Tremors were felt throughout Europe, and there were tidal surges observed as far away as Finland and at the mouth of the Elbe, not far from Kant’s otherwise quite protected Königsberg. Second, as a single rupture, it exerted a huge impact on Europe’s intellectual life. It helped to shape the entire Enlightenment. It was a formative impact on a series of thinkers, including Kant. In contrast to many other previous and later disasters in European history, the Lisbon earthquake was more than a local disaster that changed the life of the Portuguese population, then fading into oblivion (see Larsen 2006). This was a genuine cataclysm, with lasting impacts on European science, social life, religion and philosophy. It was an important event even for the history of city planning, as the reconstruction of the city turned into a full-blown experiment of rationalist urban planning. The city centre was rebuilt with a remarkable rectangular grid (considering the curved hills on which it is built); the admirably broad Avenidas (such as Avenida da Libertade) are reminiscent of the Paris boulevards. In the urban rationalization process, religious forces were stripped of their powers (and property) to vie for secular needs; at a very direct level, the event sparked the twin modern processes of rationalization and secularization (although the Church would later regain much of its property and power). Conversely, the event strengthened Rousseau in his anti-urban thinking (as he wrote in his August 18, 1756 letter to Liminality and the Modern 96 Voltaire, angrily reacting against his poem on the earthquake, ‘If the residents of this large city had been more evenly dispersed and less densely housed, the losses would have been fewer or perhaps none at all’). In October 1931, Walter Benjamin wrote a radio talk for children on the subject of the Lisbon earthquake. Here Benjamin described how the earthquake ‘excited and preoccupied the entire world like few other events in that century’ (Benjamin 2005c; here as quoted in Ray 2006: 7–8). We should understand Benjamin’s assessment well: this was the century of the French Revolution, of American Independence, and a whole series of other not-so-minor events of global significance. The Lisbon earthquake had ramifications at all levels of human life. As a single event, it shook the metaphysical optimism of Europe’s leading philosophers. According to one Voltaire scholar, the Lisbon earthquake was nothing less than ‘the death of optimism’ (Besterman as quoted in Ray: 8). Or, as Adorno put it in Negative Dialectics, ‘[t]he earthquake of Lisbon sufficed to cure Voltaire of the theodicy of Leibniz’ (1973: 361). Not without reason, Adorno invoked the earthquake under his discussion of After Auschwitz: as the Holocaust for us, in the eighteenth century it was the Lisbon earthquake which forced humans to question any easy notion of progress and optimism. Even the material effects were rapidly felt all over Europe, and they affected the economy hugely. But much more fundamentally: it affected the whole conception of how humans should think and act in a world in which such events occurred. It forced rational thought to deal with liminality. And it did so in its own very peculiar and problematic way – and this is best seen in the work of Kant. The Lisbon Earthquake and the Liminal Sources of Modern Philosophy: The Impact on Immanuel Kant Kant was twenty-nine years old when the earthquake occurred. He was a young man who had never left his hometown of Königsberg; socially challenged as he was, he would never do so in the future. It might be said that the earthquake was the one pivotal event that brought the world to Kant in all its drama, and against which he reacted by mobilizing his cognitive powers. It must be remembered that Kant’s first publication (which for reasons still under debate he did not submit as his Master’s thesis, and rather led him to drop out) was on ‘living forces’, Thoughts on the True Estimation of Living Forces. Kant’s motivating question in his early career (and it stayed with him even as it altered) was to understand what moves things in nature: which ‘forces’ are in play, how substances interact, and how we can observe and study these forces. In this work, Kant systematically and dissectingly discussed the question of natural forces and their interactions. He was interested in dynamic forces, and in what happens when forces meet and collide, with effects back to the original activity. The book was a failure; no-one took notice of it, and his critique of Descartes and Newton went largely unnoticed. Kant went for it again with a 1754 publication, where he returned with the more famous Spin-Cycle essay. As its title states, this work is an ‘investigation of the question whether the earth in its axial rotation, Dimensions of Liminality 97 whereby it causes the change of day and night, has experienced any change since the earliest times of its origin, and how one could answer this question’. Kant here describes gravitational attraction as ‘nature’s universal engine’ (das allgemeine Triebwerk der Natur). In the essay, Kant also announces his next book (1755) with the working title, ‘Cosmogony, or attempt to deduce the origin of the cosmos, the constitution of celestial bodies, and the causes of their motions, from the general laws of motion of matter according to Newton’s theory’ (Schönfeld 2012). Cosmic action turns on gravitation, the reciprocal attraction of masses. When drawn together, masses collide, crash, and are laterally deflected. The angular momentum of deflections generates a counterforce to centripetal gravitation: centrifugal repulsion. In his second book Universal Natural History and Theory of the Sky (1755), Kant sees that the pumps of the entelechy, its push and pull, are attraction and repulsion. Matter is, then, all you need, he says, ‘and you can start building a world’ (ibid.). ‘Start building a world’ – this is very much what Kant’s thought is all about, and it is this notion that later comes to underpin social constructivism. Kant’s theory also prepared the ground for today’s Big Bang theories. The pulse of the bond, Kant would move on to argue, a flaring out structure, is best imagined as fire, one of nature’s essences, and hence a component of the cosmic matrix. In fact, Kant eventually graduated with A Succinct Outline of Some Meditations on Fire, a penetration into the structure of fire. Cosmic forces, gravity, earthly rotation, interaction of elements, space-time collusion, small and big bangs, entropy, fire: those were Kant’s foundational philosophical interests. The Lisbon earthquake had it all. It erupted at the ‘right moment’, in one of Kant’s most dynamic phases, just as he was attempting to bridge his natural philosophy with a newly grounded epistemology. After his Master’s, Kant wrote his dissertation, the New Elucidation of the First Principles of Metaphysical Cognition (1755). It is no longer about force, cosmos, or fire, but about ontology, specifically the conceptual tools for metaphysical investigations. This became Kant’s ultimate motive: to explore the cognitive access to nature, the causal structure of human integration into nature. The earthquake therefore erupted right between Kant’s dissertation and his habilitationsschrift, the Physical Monadology (1756), when Kant was still working on the Physical Monadology, and just three weeks after he had given his very first lecture (which he delivered on 13 October). Kant’s Writings on the Lisbon Earthquake Kant published three essays on the catastrophe in 1756. The first, On the Causes of the Terrestrial Convulsions (January 1756), was an essay on the physical dynamics of earthquakes. The second tract, Most Remarkable Cases of the Earthquake (February 1756), is an analytical report on the earthquake which ‘shook a great
